And then just last month, I launched another SAS called Ghost or ghostriter.com. And that one's early days, 40 users, but still a fun experience nonetheless. Let's just jump into the talk on MCP. MCP has been gaining a lot of popularity over the past 10 months, or I guess 11 months now. Anthropic released it in November of 2024 and now it's kind of become maybe kind of a buzzword but also a tool that people are using in many different contexts. So if you're wondering what this is, MCP is just a standard way to connect tools and context to AI applications. The analogy that Anthropic uses to describe MCP is that it's kind of like the USBC port of AI apps. So right now I have my laptop and I've got like multiple devices plugged into it using USBC. And you can think of this in the context of AI apps. You can have your AI app like claw desktop and then you can plug in a bunch of peripheral devices into cloud desktop using MCP. Just to give you some concrete use cases of this, you can use MCP to do custom integrations to AI apps like cloud desktop. So if you wanted to give Claude access to your Google Drive and then give it access to Slack channels so it has additional context and even if you wanted it to message people, generate summaries of meetings and post it in Slack or summarize discussions and upload it to Google Drive, you can have it do that. You can also plug and play different tool sets. So you can go find MCP servers on the internet and then you can give them to large language models to build custom applications and custom agents which we'll get a little flavor of today. Or you can build your own custom MCP servers and connect them to your favorite LLM or even your favorite LLM application. The way MCP works is actually using a very common architecture that is used a lot in web development, which is this client server architecture. And so basically this is kind of like if you go to a coffee shop and you're the client and the server is like the barista. And basically what's going to happen is you're going to make some kind of request to the server. So, for example, you might request a pumpkin spice latte and then in response to your request, you'll get a response from the server. So, the server will say, "Yeah, you got it." And then they'll make you a pumpkin spice latte. This is essentially how MCP works. So, instead of a client at a coffee shop and a briea that is the server, you'll have an MCP client and an MCP server. Basically, the requests that the MCP client might make are things like asking the server what tools it has. available and then the MCP server will respond with things like the list of whatever tools it has. Let's dive into each of these parts of the MCP workflow. We'll start with the client. So, basically the client is something that's built into the AI application and it's what is going to send requests to the MCP server and receive the responses and make those responses available to the application. For example, you can have an application like claw desktop which has an LLM inside of it. And then claw desktop has this MCP client inside of it. And this is what is going to interact and communicate with the large language model. And then the MCP client is going to interact with the server. You can communicate with multiple servers and then this is going to be facilitated through multiple clients. But essentially what the client is going to be responsible for are things like discovering the server capabilities. You know, asking it what tools do you have available? What resources do you have available? What prompts are available? Receiving data from servers, getting a prompt or a resource that the server is making available. It's going to go through the client and then the client is going to decide how to put that in the context window of the large language model and also managing the tool execution because LLMs by themselves can't actually run tools. They can generate text which tells the client how it wants to execute the tool call but ultimately it's the client that has to trigger the Python code to actually be run or whatever it is. So that's the client. The other part of the MCP workflow is the server. So the server is what is going to listen for requests from the MCP clients and respond accordingly. Again we have this little example of cloud desktop MCP client talking to MCP server. And the server is going to have three key features. So the first thing it's going to have a set of tools. So this could be just like Python functions. It could be accessing an API like notion's API. It could do arbitrary things like image processing. It's going to have resources. So you can think of this as like a database, a file system. Basically things that you want the LLM or the AI application to have access to. And then finally, you can have prompts stored in the server. So these are pre-written instructions that might be helpful for various tasks. And so we're going to see some examples of this in the demo a little later. But the last thing we're talking about is basically the way you can facilitate the communication between the client and the server. So the first and simplest way you can have the client and server talk to each other is locally. So you can have everything running on your laptop and then this is facilitated by the standard IO. This is great for if you're just doing things locally or if you're trying to develop your own MCP server, you can test it locally. You can also have this communication between the client and server remotely using HTTP. The way this works is the way most things on the web work. So you'll have some application and it's going to send a request. So in this case, it's going to be a post request to some other computer on the internet that is hosting this MCP server and then they can talk to each other. So a concrete example of this is that notion has a remote MCP server. So if I'm running cloud desktop locally, I can communicate with notion's MCP server and get access to various tools and resources that are available in my notion account. And so, it's actually very important that when you are creating these tools for your MCP server, you have a very detailed dock string on how this tool actually works. because this dock string is what's going to be passed to the large language model when it's trying to decide what tool is relevant to a particular user request.
